title: Podman 5.5.1-1 segfaults when dealing with lockfiles
body:
### Issue Description

I rebooted my workstation to reboot into a new kernel and now none of my rootless podman containers will start. I can't even run podman ps -a. Linger is enabled. I am using the BTRFS storage driver.

Downgrading to 5.4.1 deals with the lock files no problem and fixed the issue. I was able to upgrade back to 5.5.1 and run my containers again.



### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. Not sure
2. Reboot
3. Log into GNOME
4. None of my containers start
5. `podman ps -a` segfaults
6. Wife yells at me because she can't watch her stories on Jellyfin :(
7. Downgrade to 5.4.1
8. Run podman version to fix the lock files
9. Upgrade back to 5.5.1

### Describe the results you received

See segfault below.

### Describe the results you expected

Many containers running.

### podman info output

```yaml
$ rpm -q podman
podman-5.5.1-1.fc42.x86_64

Podman info and version also segfault
```

### Podman in a container

No

### Privileged Or Rootless

Rootless

### Upstream Latest Release

Yes

### Additional environment details

Additional environment details

### Additional information

_No response_

From the stack trace it seems `Acquire(0x0` is called with a nil pointer and thus panics. Code wise this could only happen if we call Enqueue() before SetMaxThreads().

My would be it is triggered by the autoremoval code on setup but I don't see the error sequence yet and AFAIK we didn't do recent changes around this 
```
ERRO[0000] Unable to remove container 33e6386e0368aeab535e41e139d53b34814ec9bc4b56c3fe1ac51417493f8405 which should have autoremoved: error removing container 33e6386e0368aeab535e41e139d53b34814ec9bc4b56c3fe1ac51417493f8405 pod: pod 59f242a62c6da8df3389a0ea00252f59ad5e308740ca34c46ad79f0c0f15e335 is not empty: container already exists
```

I guess you don't how how to reliably reproduce this?
I am pretty sure I found the cause for the panic in https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/26471 but I was unable to trigger this condition here.

Unfortunatly I don't know how this happened. All I did was `dnf update` and reboot. I have a lot of containers running on my workstation via quadlets (75 of them, most of them in pods) so maybe they were not shutting down fast enough before the reboot happened?
I also I want to restate I am using the btrfs storage driver, so maybe that is a factor too?

It is 100% related to some weird state due an "unclean" shutdown. Of course next boot should be able to recover but these kind of things are really hard to test so we miss things and I don't think our normal CI suites can handle such reboot tests.

I did many hard shutdowns in my VMs and got other issues so I am sure there is quite some work left for us in that area.
As for btrfs driver itself I don't think it matters for the exact error. It blows up in our regular state refresh db code which is the same regardless of driver. That said the btrfs driver is completely untested AFAIK we only focus on overlay and vfs drivers.

Sounds good. Untested you say… good thing I like to live dangerously with my data. Thanks for your fast turn around on addressing this issue @Luap99! I really appreciate all of your hard work on Podman.
